profile.name: William
profile.role_in_household: Primary client; co-earner and tends to take point on budgeting and debt/mortgage discussions
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate-to-high: comfortable with account types and interest rates, asks for specifics, and can follow tradeoffs (taxes vs. liquidity), but doesn’t always know the fine print on every holding
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Conservative in practice: prefers protecting principal and keeping a cash buffer; gets uneasy with big swings and will ask how a recommendation behaves in a down market before agreeing
profile.communication_style: Direct and detail-oriented; will reference exact-ish numbers and dates, but still uses meeting-language like “I think it’s around…” and will pause to confirm. Asks clarifying questions and occasionally corrects small inaccuracies.
emotional_tendencies: mild anxiety around monthly cash flow
relief when plans are concrete and automated
profile.decision_making_style: Analytical and checklist-driven; prefers to compare 2–3 options, then decide and implement quickly once comfortable
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously open: willing to share details and take guidance, but wants transparency on fees, assumptions, and what could go wrong
internal_biases: anchoring to current monthly payment amounts
status quo bias (prefers small changes over big reallocations)
hidden_concerns: worries that high fixed payments could crowd out saving for retirement and the child’s education
concerned about being the one responsible if the plan doesn’t work

profile.name: Margaret
profile.role_in_household: Spouse/partner; co-earner and tends to focus on long-term security (retirement/benefits) while deferring some day-to-day number crunching
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate: understands broad concepts (retirement accounts, pensions/benefits, diversification) but prefers round numbers and may mix up terms like balance vs. payment or gross vs. net until it’s clarified
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Generally conservative but more flexible than William: can tolerate some market risk if it’s framed as goal-based and bounded (e.g., a defined portion for growth), yet dislikes feeling “locked in” or surprised
profile.communication_style: Warm and conversational; uses qualifiers like “maybe” and “I’m not totally sure,” asks for plain-English explanations, and may interrupt with practical questions about how it affects monthly life. Remembers the gist more than exact figures.
emotional_tendencies: stress when conversations get too technical
profile.decision_making_style: Collaborative and values-aligned; wants to agree as a couple and will support decisions once she feels heard and the plan matches priorities
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Moderate: builds trust through clarity and follow-through; responds well to summaries and written next steps
internal_biases: availability bias (recent market headlines influence comfort level)
hidden_concerns: concerned about health and lifestyle choices affecting long-term plans, but may not lead with it
worries about having enough flexibility for the child’s needs without feeling financially constrained
